verified_against._note: Audit methodology and per-rule anchor verification recorded in docs/internal/CONTRACT-AUDIT-1.6608.2.md.

subagent_tool_filter._description: All built-in tools pass through one filter function (`LW8` in v2.1.138 CLI bundle) parameterized by `{tools, isBuiltIn, isAsync, permissionMode}`. The function applies up to 5 layers in order: (1) MCP fast-path, (2) edit-tools-in-plan-mode, (3) drop set, (4) non-builtin extra drop set, (5) async-only allowlist. MCP tools (`name.startsWith('mcp__') || isMcp===true`) ALWAYS pass — they're never gated by the allowlists.

logic: if hG(tool) return true                     // MCP fast-path: name starts with 'mcp__' or isMcp===true
if A1(tool, ExitPlanMode) && permissionMode==='plan' return true
if isAsync && !async_allowlist.has(tool.name): { fallback then return false }

drop_set.description: Always dropped, regardless of dispatch mode. Includes EnterPlanMode/ExitPlanMode (plan-mode is gated separately), TaskOutput (parent-only), Agent (legacy alias), AskUserQuestion (interactive — no parent listening), WaitForMcpServers (init-time only).

non_builtin_extra_drop_set.description: Additional drops applied only when `isBuiltIn === false`. v2.1.138 ships `M58 = new Set([...$zH])` — same contents as drop_set; the separation exists to allow divergence in future versions.

async_dispatch_allowlist.description: When `isAsync=true`, ONLY these names pass (in addition to MCP tools and the plan-mode pass-through). Used for `/background`, `/bg`, `/fork` and other async dispatch paths.
async_dispatch_allowlist.discrepancy_resolution: Earlier internal docs (claude-code-internals L89 / gist 303b6213b7a33167b3f98b076a5f81ad) claimed 'the async filter strips Bash'. The empirical observation that Bash isn't in a Cowork sub-agent's tool list IS correct. The mechanism explanation IS NOT — `LW8`/`Ys_` does include Bash (via the `...$2 = [Bash, PowerShell]` spread). The actual gate is **`HOST_LOOP_EXCLUDED_BUILTIN_TOOLS`** (`jie` in the desktop bundle): Cowork excludes Bash, NotebookEdit, REPL, JavaScript, and WebFetch from registered built-in tools at the host-loop layer, BEFORE the sub-agent allowlist filter runs. Replacements are registered as MCP tools — `mcp__workspace__bash`, `mcp__workspace__web_fetch`. Empirically reconfirmed May 2026: an actual Cowork sub-agent reports `mcp__workspace__bash` as a *deferred* tool (loadable via ToolSearch), while built-in `Bash` is absent. A host-CLI (CCD-mode) probe DOES show Bash in async sub-agents — because CCD mode doesn't apply the host-loop tool exclusion. The spec must distinguish these two layers; see `host_loop_tool_substitution`.

experimental_fallback_allowlist.description: When the async filter would reject a tool but `experimentalAgentTeams` flag is enabled (`r9() && PW()`), additional tool names pass.
experimental_fallback_allowlist.gate: r9() && PW() — both must return truthy. Resolves to a feature-flag check; not enabled by default.

fork_subagent_allowlist.description: v2.1.138 introduces a separate, more restrictive allowlist `Fp9` for fork-style subagents (the implicit `kind:'fork'` agent). This is distinct from `Ys_` (async). Three empty spreads (`...[]`) suggest conditionally-populated members under feature flags not yet active.

sync_task_tool.description: Sync Task-tool sub-agent dispatch is the same `LW8` filter with `isAsync=false`. The `Ys_` allowlist gate is skipped — only the drop_set and non_builtin_extra_drop_set apply. Sync sub-agents see effectively all built-in tools EXCEPT the 6 in the drop set, plus all MCP tools.
sync_task_tool.effective_filter: drop_set + non_builtin_extra_drop_set only; allowlist not applied
sync_task_tool.behaviorally_includes: everything else from the master tool registry, plus all MCP tools

host_loop_tool_substitution._description: Operates in the desktop bundle (`Claude.app/.../index.js`), not the CLI. When the desktop spawns a Cowork session, it filters its registered built-in tools through a host-loop-safe set BEFORE handing them to the SDK. Tools in `HOST_LOOP_EXCLUDED_BUILTIN_TOOLS` are dropped; some are replaced via the desktop-registered `workspace` MCP server (see `replacements`); others are dropped without any replacement (see `host_loop_dropped_builtins`). This is THE gate that makes `Bash` empirically unavailable to Cowork sub-agents, not the CLI's `LW8`/`Ys_` filter.
host_loop_tool_substitution.scope: Cowork mode only (Claude.app spawning `claude --cowork` inside microVM). CCD mode (host CLI without `--cowork`) does NOT apply this substitution — Bash is registered normally in CCD.

vm_substrate_by_platform.macos: Apple Hypervisor with bundled disk image (`Claude.app/Contents/Resources/smol-bin.{x64,arm64}.img`). VM lifecycle managed in-process by Claude.app.
vm_substrate_by_platform.windows: Hyper-V via separately-installed `CoworkVMService` (Windows service running `cowork-svc.exe`). Requires `vmcompute` and `HvHost` services. Service autostart has known reliability issues — see https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/56772 (and 48661, 48001, 55649).
vm_substrate_by_platform.linux: <not yet traced — likely KVM-based; check Linux build of Claude.app>

windows_service_stopped.user_visible_error: Workspace unavailable. The isolated Linux environment failed to start.
windows_service_stopped.actual_cause: CoworkVMService stopped or failed to autostart.
windows_service_stopped.impact: mcp__workspace__bash dies; file-op tools (Read/Write/Edit/Glob/Grep) and request_cowork_directory continue to work because they don't depend on the VM.
windows_service_stopped.manual_recovery: Start-Service CoworkVMService (DACL allows interactive user; no elevation needed).

host_loop_safe_set._description: Tools that survive host-loop filtering. PTi(tools) = tools.filter(t => t.startsWith('mcp__') || zvt.includes(t)). MCP tools always pass; built-ins must be in this set.

host_loop_excluded_builtins._description: Built-in tool names that the desktop EXPLICITLY EXCLUDES from registration in Cowork mode. These are the names the desktop bundle considers 'unsafe to run on the host loop'. Two get an MCP replacement (see `replacements`); three are dropped without replacement (see `host_loop_dropped_builtins`).

mcp_replacements._note_others: NotebookEdit, REPL, JavaScript have NO mcp__*__* replacement in the v1.6608.2 desktop registry. The earlier prose conjecture (`mcp__workspace__notebookedit` etc.) was fictional. CW001/CW002 should distinguish 'replaced' from 'dropped' — see siblings `replacements` and `host_loop_dropped_builtins` for the canonical split.

replacements._description: The subset of `host_loop_excluded_builtins` that the desktop's `workspace` MCP server replaces with an `mcp__workspace__*` tool. Note: `WebFetch` maps to `mcp__workspace__web_fetch` (snake_case), NOT `mcp__workspace__webfetch`. Source of truth for CW001/CW002 'use mcp__workspace__X instead of X' suggestions.
replacements.verified_against: claude.app@1.6608.2 desktop bundle (.vite/build/index.js); enumeration of `mcp__workspace__<tool>` literals.

host_loop_dropped_builtins._description: The subset of `host_loop_excluded_builtins` that the desktop drops without registering any MCP replacement. CW001/CW002 should suggest 'avoid in Cowork — no equivalent' rather than 'use mcp__workspace__X' for these names. Includes interactive notebook/REPL tools and the (server-side) JavaScript runtime — meaningful only in main-loop dispatch, not in async sub-agents.
host_loop_dropped_builtins.verified_against: claude.app@1.6608.2 desktop bundle (.vite/build/index.js); confirmed by exhaustive search for `mcp__workspace__<x>` and `mcp__cowork__<x>` strings — none present for these three names.

cowork_builtin_mcp_servers._description: MCP server prefixes that the Cowork desktop auto-registers — i.e. tools whose names start with `mcp__<server>__` are runtime-supplied built-ins, not user-authored. CW009 should treat any `mcp__<server>__*` reference where `<server>` is in this list as a recognised name, not a typo. Source of truth: structural enumeration of `mcp__<server>__<tool>` literals in the v1.6608.2 desktop bundle.
cowork_builtin_mcp_servers._note_v1_6259_1: Earlier docs in RULES.md/SPEC.md listed only 3 servers (`workspace`, `cowork`, `cowork-onboarding`). v1.6608.2 ships 6 additional ones (`mcp-registry`, `plugins`, `radar`, `scheduled-tasks`, `skills`, `terminal`). CW009 was silently false-positiving the missing six prior to this contract refresh.

renderer_dependent_extra_drops._description: Additional drops applied when the session is a 'Bridge' or 'Dispatch child' (i.e. async sub-agent dispatch). On top of host-loop exclusion.
renderer_dependent_extra_drops.trigger: Bridge session OR Dispatch child session — see `(a||c)` in LocalAgentModeSessionManager log line 'Bridge session: disabling renderer-dependent tools'

deferred_tools_tier._description: Cowork exposes some tools in a 'deferred' tier — name visible in the registry, schema loaded on demand via ToolSearch. `mcp__workspace__bash` is the canonical example: it doesn't appear in a sub-agent's immediate tool list, but ToolSearch with query 'bash' or 'select:mcp__workspace__bash' loads its schema, after which it's callable. Skill authors targeting Cowork should declare deferred tools and use ToolSearch as a precondition for calling them.
deferred_tools_tier.implication_for_skill_authors: Probing 'tools available to my Cowork sub-agent' must enumerate BOTH the immediate set AND the deferred tier (queryable via ToolSearch). Tools missing from the immediate list may still be callable.

kernel_env_passthrough.description: Host env vars that survive into the Cowork VM kernel shell (bash/python/R). Vars not in this allowlist are dropped before kernel spawn.

extra_env_override_path.description: extraEnv argument bypasses the allowlist post-filter. Populated only by the user_secrets pipeline — see `user_secrets_injection`.

user_secrets_injection.description: The Operon kernel-secrets subsystem (encrypted SQLite store, IPC bridge, name-validation regex). NOTE: empirically removed from the v1.6608.2 desktop bundle (zero `OperonSecrets` / `claude.operon` occurrences). Entry retained verbatim for historical reference and so CW010's anchors still resolve while it is being demoted/re-anchored — see Task B4. The successor (extension manifest `user_config`) has different validation and is not yet enumerated here.
user_secrets_injection._followup: Task B4 demotes CW010 to `info` (hygiene-only retention). Replacement validation rules for the new `user_config` system (zod schemas `eqt`/`cqt`/`wqt`) will be enumerated in a future contract refresh.

renderer_bridge.origin_check: y() — top frame at https://claude.ai or https://preview.claude.ai

storage.encryption: Electron safeStorage (macOS Keychain bundle ACL com.anthropic.claudefordesktop)

session_kinds.description: Discriminator on CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_KIND. The CLI reads this to route session behavior; skill authors should not assume kind='bg' implies kind='fork' or vice versa.
session_kinds._note: ar() in v2.1.138 returns the env value only if it is exactly 'bg', 'daemon', or 'daemon-worker'; anything else returns undefined. N7() === ar() === 'bg' is the public predicate for 'is this a background session'.

bg_context_env_strip.description: Two distinct strip lists are applied at different boundaries. List A is used when re-entering a non-BG context from a BG session (clean parent env). List B is used when spawning a BG spare worker, to remove terminal/IDE-detection vars so the worker doesn't think it's interactive.

list_a_explicit_deletes._note_on_count: Earlier internal docs cited '5-var BG-context env-strip'. That count was for an older version; v2.1.138 has 9 explicit + OTEL_*.

list_b_terminal_strip_for_spare_worker.description: Used by `xr3()` when spawning a BG spare worker. Strips terminal/IDE-detection env so the worker doesn't render interactive UI.
list_b_terminal_strip_for_spare_worker._note: Truncated in this trace (~30+ entries with conditional spreads `...[]`). Full extraction needed.

bg_context_env_set.description: Vars SET (not stripped) when launching a BG worker.

conditional.CLAUDE_BG_ISOLATION: set to 'worktree' when worktree-isolation mode is enabled

secret_unset_list.description: OPERON_SECRET_VARS — names unset by python/R kernel bootstrap before user code runs. NOT an output scrubber. Output scrubbing uses decrypted scrubValues separately.
secret_unset_list._status_v1_6608_2: carried_forward_from_v1_6259_1; OperonSecrets subsystem itself appears removed from desktop bundle, but the OPERON_SECRET_VARS literal list anchor is unchanged from the prior contract — re-verify in next refresh.

skill_frontmatter_invariants.description: Skill manifest fields the runtime checks at load time. Violating these will silently disable the skill or change its dispatch path. NOTE: this contract distinguishes the desktop manifest-display layer (4 fields: name, description, argument-hint, user-invocable, accessed via desktop's `dh(r,...)` helper) from the CLI runtime-parser layer (5 fields enumerated in `runtime_parser_fields_v2_1_138`). CW004/CW005 anchor on the CLI layer, not the desktop layer.

forbidden_fields.reason: When set to true, the CLI runtime parser (claude-code-cli@2.1.138) blocks model-driven skill invocation entirely. Enforcement site: `if (z.disableModelInvocation && !tE7(O,_)) return skill_invoke_model_disabled` in the v2.1.138 bundle; the kebab-case key is normalised to camelCase by the same parser. Round-1 audit incorrectly concluded the field was no longer recognised because the desktop's `dh(r,...)` accessor (manifest *display* layer) doesn't read it — round-3 verification corrected that: the actual frontmatter→runtime parser is in the CLI bundle, where the field has 5+ kebab-form occurrences and 13+ camel-form occurrences including the enforcement site above.
forbidden_fields.verified_against: claude-code-cli@2.1.138 — kebab-case `disable-model-invocation` (5 lines), camelCase `disableModelInvocation` (13 lines), enforcement string `skill_invoke_model_disabled` (1 line). Desktop bundle claude.app@1.6608.2 has 0 kebab-form occurrences, confirming the CLI is the authoritative anchor.

env_var_substitution.reason: Bare form depends on shell-expansion timing not guaranteed for skill subprocesses. The runtime substitutes the braced form via `H.replace(/\$\{CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT\}/g, () => q(_.path))`; the bare form is left as a literal and only resolves when the executing context happens to perform shell expansion (so it works in `bash -c` argv but silently fails in argv-only invocations). The same pattern applies to `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}` (parallel substitution regex) — bare `$CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA` has the same silent-failure surface. CW003 flags bare forms of both env vars.

runtime_parser_fields_v2_1_138._description: All skill-frontmatter fields the CLI bundle 2.1.138 normalises and reads at runtime, with the count of occurrences in the bundle. This is distinct from the desktop manifest-display layer (which only reads `name`, `description`, `argument-hint`, `user-invocable`). The contract enumerates both layers explicitly so future audits can spot divergence.

fields.name: allowed-tools
fields._followup: Task C4 — investigate semantics; possibly CW013.

fields.name: force-for-plugin
fields._followup: Task C4 — investigate semantics; possibly CW014.

fields.name: keep-coding-instructions
fields._followup: Task C4 — investigate semantics; possibly CW015.

manifest_display_fields_v1_6608_2._description: Fields the desktop's `dh(r, '<field>')` accessor reads. This is the manifest *display* layer (skill chooser, settings UI), distinct from the CLI runtime parser. Fields not in this list are still load-bearing at runtime if the CLI parser reads them.

cli_launch_args_in_cowork._description: Args the desktop passes when spawning the in-VM CLI. These constrain what the CLI loads, beyond what the bundle's runtime gates do. Verified by inspecting the [Spawn:create] log line in `~/Library/Logs/Claude/cowork_vm_node.log` (or `%APPDATA%\Claude\logs\cowork_vm_node.log` on Windows).
cli_launch_args_in_cowork._note_setting_sources_v2_1_138: The literal `--setting-sources=user` is a per-version simplification. In CLI 2.1.138 the actual flag formation is `--setting-sources=${<ident>.join(',')}` where the joined array is one of `['user','local','project']` or `['user','project','local']`. Neither variant contains `'plugin'`, so the consequence below (plugin hooks excluded) holds — only the literal text is outdated. CW011 anchors on the consequence, not the literal.

per_plugin._note: One per installed plugin. Path resolved to the VM-side mount under /sessions/<id>/mnt/.local-plugins/cache/<marketplace>/<plugin>/<version>/ or .remote-plugins/<plugin-id>/.

plugin_hooks_excluded.description: `--setting-sources=...` restricts settings resolution to scopes that never include `plugin`. Plugin-scoped hooks (declared in a plugin's `hooks/hooks.json`) are silently excluded from hook discovery — they DO NOT FIRE in Cowork sessions. Plugin skills, slash commands, and MCP servers still load via the per-plugin `--plugin-dir` args, but the hook lifecycle is dead from the plugin's perspective.
plugin_hooks_excluded.scope: All Cowork dispatch levels (top-level main session and forked sub-agents).
plugin_hooks_excluded.verified_empirically: 8 MB of recent cowork_vm_node.log + 2 MB coworkd.log show zero 'hook' references for any Cowork session (named pattern <adj>-<adj>-<word>); the same desktop process logs `[Stop hook] Query completed` for CCD sessions (pattern local_<uuid>) on the same host.

upstream_tracking.title: Plugin hooks not loaded from external hooks.json file
upstream_tracking.scope: general CLI race — most hook dispatchers call hook execution without `await loadPluginHooks()` first; affects CCD intermittently

upstream_tracking.title: Cowork: Plugin hooks from hooks/hooks.json never fire — --setting-sources user excludes plugin scope
upstream_tracking.scope: Cowork-specific scope exclusion via the launch flag — separate from #16288's race; even fixing the race won't help Cowork until the launch flag is changed too

plugin_hooks_excluded.workaround: Move hooks from `<plugin>/hooks/hooks.json` to `~/.claude/settings.json` (user scope). Loads in both Cowork and CCD.
